content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance
(916) 322-5662

Administration
322-5660

Executive/Legal
322-5901

Enforcement
322-6441

Statements of Economic Interest
322-6444

June 23, 1983


Larry Remer
San Diego Newsline
3651 Fourth Avenue
San Diego, CA  92103

Re: Advice Letter No. A-83-144

Dear Mr. Remer:

     Thank you for your letter requesting advice regarding
possible conflicts of interest between your professional
activities and your position as a member of the California
Public Broadcasting Commission (CPBC). You are a professional
journalist; you write for several publications, and regularly
appear on commercial television. In addition, you own a weekly
newspaper. The newspaper sells advertising space, and, as a
sideline, also performs commercial graphics work for a variety
of customers.

     The Political Reform Act requires a public official to
disqualify himself from making or participating in any
governmental decision in which he has a financial interest.
Government Code Section 87100.1/  It does not limit the
nongovernmental activities of a public official in any way.2/
Therefore, the Political Reform Act does not prevent you from
engaging in any of the professional activities described in your
letter. However, because it is possible that you may be
required to disqualify yourself from certain decisions of the
CPBC as a result of these activities, I will discuss the

1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise stated.

2/  There are some other laws, notably Section 1090, which
contain additional prohibitions on the activities of public
officials. You may check with your agency counsel or the
Attorney General for advice on these provisions.

Larry Remer
June 23, 1983
Page 2


disqualification provisions of the Act briefly as they apply to
you.2/

     Disqualification4/ is required under Section 87100 as to
any CPBC decision in which you have a "financial interest."  A
financial interest is defined in Section 87103:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more
than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

                         * * *

     (c)  Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

     Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Political Reform Act may
require you to disqualify yourself from CPBC decisions affecting
your newspaper, affecting public broadcasting stations which
have been sources of income to you in the past 12 months, or
affecting your own personal income.

_______________________

     3/  I have also enclosed a Commission pamphlet on
conflicts of interest for your information.

     4/  When you are required to disqualify yourself from a
decision you may not vote on, participate in, or use your
official position to influence the decision.  Section 87100;
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (copy enclosed).

Larry Remer
June 23, 1983
Page 3


                    Decisions Affecting Your Newspaper

     Although you did not state the value of your investment in
your newspaper, I assume it exceeds $1,000. Therefore, under
Section 87103(a), you have a financial interest in a CPBC
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on your newspaper. For
example, if a CPBC decision would have a material5/ financial
effect on your newspaper's income from paid advertising,
disqualification would be required.

               Decisions Affecting Sources of Income

     From your letter, it appears that you receive income as a
journalist from public broadcasting stations. If you have
received $250 or more from any one station in the past 12
months, that station is a "source of income" to you. If it is
reasonably foreseeable that any CPBC decision will have a
material6/ financial effect on such a station, you must
disqualify yourself from that decision.

     Even if you do not receive income directly from a public
broadcasting station, the station may be considered a source of
income to you if it has paid your newspaper for advertising or
other services in the past year. If you own 10% or more of the
newspaper, a pro rata share of all gross income to the newspaper
is income to you.7/  Section 82030(a). If your pro rata share
of the gross income to your newspaper over the past 12 months
from a public broadcasting station has been $250 or more, that
station is a source of income to you. If it is reasonably
foreseeable that a CPBC decision will have a material financial
effect on such a broadcasting station, you must disqualify
yourself from the decision.

_______________________

     5/  Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(1) defines when an effect on a business entity such as
a newspaper is "material." (Copy enclosed.)

     6/  2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1).

     7/  If you own 100% of the newspaper, all income received
by the newspaper is treated as if it had been paid directly to
you.

Larry Remer
June 23, 1983
Page 4


                    Decisions Affecting Your Personal Income

     If it is reasonably foreseeable that a CPBC decision would
have a direct and material financial effect on your personal
income3/, as a journalist, you must, of course, disqualify
yourself from that decision.

     I hope that this letter has been helpful. If you have a
question about whether you may participate in any particular
CPBC decision, do not hesitate to ask us for further advice.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:pla
Enclosure


     3/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(A).

SAN DIEGO
NEWSLINE
1 June 1983

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 507
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear sir or madam:

I am a member of the CALIFORNIA PUBLIC BROADCASTING COMMISSION,
appointed by the governor to a five year term.

The COMMISSION is governed, as are most state agencies, by a
conflict of interest code established by your body. I have filed
a Form 730 as required by law.

In my profession, I am a journalist--both broadcast and print; and
I own an interest in weekly newspaper. The newspaper, as a sideline,
performs commercial graphics work (typesetting, etc.) for a variety
of customers.

I wish to address the following questions to the FPPC regarding potential
conflicts in my performance of my duties with the COMMISSION:

--As a journalist, I write for several publications and I appear
regularly on commercial television. Am I barred from appearing for
pay on any public broadcasting outlet in the state of California
that receives funding from the COMMISSION? (I used to appear on
a weekly show on UPS-TV in San Diego several years ago.)

--As a journalist, can I participate in and receive money for the
production of programs or documentaries that appear on California
public broadcasting stations?

--Can the newspaper in which I own an interest accept paid advertising
from a public broadcasting station that receives funding from the
COMMISSION?

--Can the newspaper in which I own an interest accept paid advertising
from a cable company given the role that the COMMISSION has played in
studying cable television and making recommendations about cable regulation
to the legislature?

--Can the same newspaper perform graphics work (typesetting, etc.) for
either a public broadcasting station or a cable station?

I want to stress that none of the above issues involve a situation that
already exists, only potential pitfalls I can see coming (especially with
the advertising, about which I needed to know as soon as possible).

I can provide any other information you might require.

Thank you.

Yours truly,

EDITORIAL OFFICES
3651 FOURTH AVENUE SAN DIEGO, CA 92103 PHONE: 295-0085
ADVERTISING/GRAPHICS
3609 FOURTH AVENUE SAN DIEGO, CA 92103 PHONE: 295-2134

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

June 8, 1983

Larry Remer
San Diego Newsline
3651 Fourth Avenue
San Diego, CA 92103

Re: A-83-144

Dear Mr. Remer:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to me. If you have any
questions about your advice request, please contact me
directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

sections.conclusion: Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Political Reform Act may require you to disqualify yourself from CPBC decisions affecting your newspaper, affecting public broadcasting stations which have been sources of income to you in the past 12 months, or affecting your own personal income. The Political Reform Act does not prevent you from engaging in any of the professional activities described in your letter. However, you must disqualify yourself from decisions in which you have a financial interest as defined in Section 87103.
sections.question_synthetic: Does Larry Remer's professional journalism activities, including ownership of a newspaper and receipt of income from public broadcasting stations, create conflicts of interest that would prevent him from serving on the California Public Broadcasting Commission?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the Political Reform Act does not prohibit Remer's professional activities, but he must disqualify himself from CPBC decisions affecting his newspaper, public broadcasting stations that are sources of income to him, or his personal income, as defined under Section 87103.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is clear FPPC advice letter with well-defined question and conclusion sections. OCR quality is good with only minor typos. Letter provides detailed guidance on disqualification provisions applicable to the requestor's specific circumstances. Includes statutory references and regulatory citations.

The Political Reform Act does not prevent you from engaging in any of the professional activities described in your letter. However, you must disqualify yourself from decisions in which you have a financial interest as defined in Section 87103.

embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter A-83-144 addresses conflicts of interest for Larry Remer, a journalist and newspaper owner serving on the California Public Broadcasting Commission. The FPPC concludes that while the Political Reform Act does not prohibit his professional activities, he must disqualify himself from specific CPBC decisions where he has a defined financial interest under Government Code Section 87103.
